facts_disclosed: Planning barn conversion for honey processing

turns.content: Good afternoon. I wonder if you might help me think through a rather ambitious idea I have been turning over in my mind.
Good afternoon! I'd be happy to help you think through an idea. What have you been considering?

Well, I have an old stone barn at the back of the property. It has not been used for anything meaningful in years, just storing tools and whatnot. I have been thinking quite seriously about turning it into a dedicated honey processing space. Proper extraction equipment, a bottling line, temperature control, all of it set up correctly.

That sounds like a wonderful project! A dedicated honey processing room would certainly be a step up from a makeshift setup. Stone barns can be great candidates for conversion too — the thick walls offer natural insulation, which helps with temperature stability during extraction and bottling. What's the barn like at the moment? Do you have a rough idea of the size, and is it structurally sound?

It is structurally quite sound, I should think. I spent thirty-five years as a civil engineer, so I have given it a reasonably competent once-over. The walls are solid Cotswold stone, the roof timbers need some attention in places but nothing catastrophic. I would estimate it is roughly six metres by nine. More than enough room for what I have in mind.

Six by nine metres is a very workable footprint for a honey processing room — that's about 54 square metres, which gives you plenty of space to set up distinct zones. With your engineering background, you're well placed to plan this out properly. Typically, a well-designed honey house would have a few key areas: a receiving and uncapping station near the entrance, the extraction area with your centrifuge in the centre, a settling and filtering zone, and then a bottling and labelling station. You'd also want a wash-down area and storage for equipment. Have you thought about what level of production you're aiming for? That will affect what equipment makes sense.

At the moment I manage twelve hives, and I sell at the village market. It is a modest operation, but doing everything in the kitchen has become rather impractical. Margaret would have had something to say about that, I suspect. The idea would be to have a space where I can extract, settle, bottle and label without turning the house upside down each time. Perhaps even enough capacity to take on a few more hives if the mood strikes.

That makes a lot of sense. Twelve hives producing in the kitchen is certainly doable but far from ideal, especially when it comes to hygiene standards and simply having the space to work comfortably. For your scale, you wouldn't need industrial equipment, but a proper setup would make a real difference. I'd suggest thinking about:

- A **radial extractor** that can handle 9-12 frames at a time — much more efficient than a tangential one for your volume
- A **double sieve and settling tank** with a honey gate, so you can filter and decant cleanly
- A **stainless steel bottling tank** with a valve, which makes filling jars much tidier than ladling
- A **warming cabinet** for decrystallising honey in winter

For the barn itself, you'd want a food-safe epoxy floor coating that's easy to wash down, good drainage, and ideally mains water plumbed in. Would you be looking to do the conversion yourself, or bring in a builder?

I could manage a good deal of the interior fitting myself, but the roof repairs and any plumbing or electrics would need a professional. There is a chap in the village who does building work, so I would get a quote from him. The floor coating is a good thought. I had been wondering about that. The existing floor is just earth and old flagstones.

The flagstones underneath could actually work in your favour if they're level — you could lay a self-levelling screed over them and then apply the epoxy coating on top. That would give you a smooth, food-safe, washable surface without having to dig anything out. If the flagstones are too uneven, you might need a concrete overslip first, but that's straightforward work.

For the electrics, you'd want to plan for:
- Enough sockets for the extractor motor, warming cabinet, and any lighting
- Good overhead lighting — LED panels are ideal for a workspace like this
- A dedicated circuit if you're running a warming cabinet and extractor simultaneously

And for plumbing, even a basic setup with a utility sink and hot water would make cleanup enormously easier. Have you looked into whether you'd need planning permission for the change of use? Converting an agricultural building to food processing sometimes triggers that, depending on your local planning authority.

That is the part I have been putting off, if I am honest. Planning permission. The Cotswolds being an Area of Outstanding Natural Beauty adds layers of complication. I shall have to look into it. One does rather hope that turning a barn into a honey room is the sort of thing they would approve, but one never knows with these things.

You're right to flag that — the AONB designation does add an extra layer of scrutiny. The good news is that if you're not changing the external appearance of the barn significantly (no new windows, no major structural alterations to the exterior), it's often treated more leniently. A change of use from agricultural storage to food processing is sometimes covered under permitted development rights, but in an AONB that's more restricted.

I'd recommend:
1. **Check your local planning portal** (Cotswold District Council) for their guidance on barn conversions and change of use in the AONB
2. **Consider a pre-application enquiry** — most councils offer this for a small fee, and it gives you informal guidance before you commit to a full application
3. **Keep detailed notes** on what you plan to change and what you plan to keep — demonstrating that the character of the building is preserved goes a long way

Your engineering background will actually serve you well here — you'll be able to present clear, well-documented plans, which planning officers appreciate.

A pre-application enquiry is a sensible suggestion. I shall look into that. It would be a great pity to go to all the trouble of getting quotes and ordering equipment only to be told one cannot proceed. I should rather like to have the whole thing settled before the summer, so that I could be using it by the autumn extraction.

That's a reasonable timeline if things go smoothly. A pre-application enquiry typically takes 4-6 weeks for a response. If the feedback is positive and you move to a full application, that's usually an 8-week determination period for a straightforward case, though it can stretch longer. So if you submit the pre-application soon, you could potentially have planning sorted by late May or June, leaving the summer months for the building work.

In the meantime, there's nothing stopping you from getting quotes from your builder and researching equipment suppliers. That way, if planning comes through, you can move quickly. For extraction equipment, companies like Thorne, Maisemore, and National Bee Supplies all offer good mid-range setups suitable for your scale. Worth getting a few catalogues in.

Quite right. I shall make a start on the pre-application this week. It would make the whole operation considerably more efficient, and rather more dignified than balancing a centrifuge on the kitchen worktop. Thank you for helping me think it through. It feels more achievable now that I have set it out properly.

You're very welcome. It sounds like a well-considered project, and with your engineering eye for detail, I'm sure you'll put together a thorough plan. The image of a centrifuge on the kitchen worktop is quite something — your bees deserve a proper facility. Best of luck with the pre-application, and do come back if you need help with any of the technical details or want to talk through the equipment options once you have a clearer picture from the council.
